speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, John Bond talked about it, about that there was a fault ran from St. Mary's on down along the Potlatch, along the Potlatch Canyon, and that it runs on down to the Salmon River. And I'm not a geologist, so I really don't know all about it. But one day we stood on the rim of the little Potlatch Canyon. They told me to look up the way there, and I could look down and see where the earth sank down, sort of along there, along the little, along the big Potlatch. And he says that there is a fault there that runs from St. Mary's to the Salmon River. Now that would be an interesting thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think John Miller talked about that in his book, did he?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No, I don't think so.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Now that'd be an interesting thing to look up about, wouldn't it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, I've heard about those ice caves too. I've heard that there are some, right, between Troy and Kendrick. Yes, I've heard about that too. Well, it seems like there's this geological fault that runs along there. And I think that might be interesting, because I'm not very good at this earth science, as you know. And I think that's something the kids would be very interested in exploring, about how this fault runs right along through their country. I want to ask you about the play parties. Oh, yes, the play parties. Well, it was customary on Friday evening in the rural schools. The coming of the pioneers, there was very little entertainment. There were no shows, there were no cars, there was no television, there was no radio. So they all had to make their own fun. So each person brought to the community their own ethnic background. The people from different countries brought the singing games of their land. And the people coming here, of course, to this country, they came from other states that had been settled. And each one brought in various forms of pioneer games. And that was one of the chief forms of amusement. It was the rough house of those days. You went down to the schoolhouse, you didn't drink beer, but you played party games. And it was a place for the young men to choose their partners. And this has been going on, of course, since the days of Adam and Eve. So they had all kinds of games they'd play, and most of them were something about choosing partners. And if you weren't allowed to dance at least, they could swing them around a little bit. And that served its purpose. And some of the games they played were, of course, Happy is the Miller Boy who lives by the nail, and the wheel goes round of its own free will, one hand in the hopper and the other in the sack. And we'll all turn together, and we'll all turn back. Everybody reverses, and the boy that's next to the girl grabs him a partner. And another one went like this. We're sailing east, we're sailing west, we're sailing over the ocean. And say, young man, if you want to get a wife, you've got to be quick in the motion. And they grabbed the girl nearest them, and away they went. And of course there was Skip to Maloo, my darling, with all its various verses. They must be, oh, 46 verses to it all together. And everybody that came from a different section of the country could add a few verses as it was sung in their country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was Skip to Maloo played?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Do you remember how they... Yes, they all got in a big circle. And you chose partners, and you stood and held hands in this circle. And they sang about, my wife wears a number ten shoe, my wife wears a number ten shoe, my wife wears a number ten shoe, Skip to Maloo, my darling. And then you took a hold of your girlfriend, and you swung her in a circle round and round and round, you know. And then they'd get in a big circle again. And then when it came to, we'll all turn together and we'll all turn back. One stepped ahead, the boy would step ahead, and the girl would fall back, and that gave you each a new partner. And they played lots of other choosing partner games. That was the best. And it was a chance for the young people to meet together and have fun under the very watchful eyes and chaperone of the old folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, they did this without music?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: They sang. And they all mostly sang. Of course, in some of the very early, even the earliest people brought some forms of music like violins and some school houses, but the first thing they would buy would be a little weed organ. And with someone pumping on the organ, you got a little rhythm and a little beat to it, you know. But if they didn't have anything, they could still sing and clap, you know, clap in rhythm. And that was all they needed. When young people get together, you know, they can entertain themselves without much artificial music and bass. And they played guitars, even in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, yes. Lots of them had guitars. And they would play guitars and sang. But I thought, when they call it play parties, this is to mean that it isn't a dance. Oh, well, no, it was these play party games were, there were other games, too, that they played. They would play games like Spiddin' the Platter, and they'd get out and they'd twirl a pipe in around in the floor, and then they'd call out the name of some girl and she'd run and see if she could catch it. And if she caught it, maybe she would get a prize, or if she didn't catch it. And she had to be the twirler for the next game. And they played charades. And everybody would act out something, you know, and you acted out different games. And they would play, and they played games like, and if you could guess it, that'd be fun. And if not, you had to pay a forfeit. And some of the forfeits, you know, would be like kissing games. Some very pretty girl, she would be in great demand, and she'd have to give a forfeit of kissing some other boys there. And that caused great hilarity and great fun. That was okay. That was okay, yes, that was just fine, even in those days. So you see, they had a little something going on in those days. But I thought that in some places, they played these play party games instead of dancing. They didn't approve of the regular kind of dance. Yes, well, that was right. Suddenly, you could always get by playing, and skipped a Maloo, and London Bridge is falling down. And happy is the Miller boy. If they wouldn't allow you to dance, at least you could play these games, and then you got to swing your partners. And having a girl in your arms tonight was just about the same whether you were dancing Black Bottoms or if you were playing Miller Boy. Black Bottoms. That was some kind of a dance they used to dance back many years ago. Something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they call them now? What other dances the kids do now? It isn't what to see anymore, is it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No, and it's not Charleston. That was in my age. I belonged to the Flapper age, and everybody was out doing the Charleston, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But now, do they do the Big Apple?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No, that's out of style, too. No, they just stand there and move around. They just stand there and make motions. Make suggestive motions. Let's put it that way. They just stand there and go through suggestive motions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, this was usually associated with the schoolhouse?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, yes. Of course, they did meet in each other's homes, too. But the schoolhouse had more room, and you had more freedom. The schoolhouse generally had little better lights than they had in their own private homes. Can the light in one little kerosene lamp went very conducive to big crowds. But the schoolhouse often had a gasoline lantern or something like that to light it up. And the young people then, I like the young people now, they didn't need too much light. They enjoyed it anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And who are we to say who had the most fun, you know? You think they had lots of fun in those?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, yes. Yes, I have a bunch of letters from folks I talked 50 years ago. And every one of them was telling me how much they missed the good old times they had in the good old days. The good parties that they used to have and the schoolhouses were in use every day of the week. Saturday night, they had their big play parties, and on Sunday, they had the Sunday school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This would be, say, every Saturday night?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, just practically, yes. Practically, they'd have surprise parties in each other's houses, too. If the schools got too busy with their scheduled things, then people would open their homes, and the young folks and the old folks and the little folks would go to each other's houses and have a party.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would these sometimes last all night?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, I think so, yes. I can remember getting drifted in in one of my early school teaching days. I remember the snowmen drifting so badly, we couldn't get home if we wanted to, so we just stayed all night and then went home in the morning. You know the song about, and we danced all night to broad daylight, didn't go home with the girls in the morning. So they had little all -night parties in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it usually during the winter?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Usually in the winter, because in the summer, everybody was so concerned with putting in crops and harvesting the crops and that sort of thing. They didn't have time for much sociability.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this made it a way of getting together, passing the time?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: That's right. For the long winter evenings, it broke the monotony.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, were there other things that would be a part of the program?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh, yes. Some, yes. Sometimes the schools would have what they called a basket supper, and all the girls made pretty baskets and brought them. And it was a sort of a charity thing. If they needed some new records, or if they needed a Victrola, if they needed even just some new lamps, they would have a benefit. And the girls would bring the suppers, and the boys would have to bid on them on the baskets to see who would get to eat with the girl of their choice. And the money would go to the school. And they had pie suppers. Every girl would bake a pie and bring them much spirited bidding among the married men, as well as the single men, and go to the man that didn't buy his own wife's pie. I'll tell you, that was bad. That was bad. And those were some of the other things. Well, now, let's say that they were going to have a play party night at school. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they have things that going on that night at the school?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, yes. Often the school children would start out with their program. They'd have like a little Thanksgiving program, maybe. And each would have a little recitation, maybe a little play about the coming of the pilgrims and about why we should be thankful. And when that was over, then the other folks would shove back the chairs and maybe play singing games, as they called them, or maybe play some play party games. And they'd have lunch, and then they'd go home. And that was the social life of the rural schools.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were singing games the same as by far?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, the singing games were. Well, they were the ones that you sang to. And play party games could be anything like the Spin the Platter and Post Office and musical chairs or Cross Christians and Silly Answers and all those kind of games. They, for you didn't sing, the guessing games and that sort of thing, or the singing games. Well, that could be play party games. Oh, yes, yes. Lots of people call play party games. They think of it only in terms of singing games, but that's not so. There were lots of other party games where you didn't necessarily sing, like musical chairs and like Spin the Platter forfeits, anything where you had to guess something and get it wrong. And that was the paying of a forfeit for being wrong. Where do these plays come from, that the kids would - Sometimes they would make them up, and those were the best of all if they had a little history that they were interested in and could write a little pilgrim play. And each of some people could be the Indians and some pilgrims and others, something else, you know, and if they wrote their own and what they thought they said, that would show a lot of imagination and creativity. And they learned to spell incidentally and use proper grammar, and then they dramatized it and put it on for the benefit of their folks. Sometimes they just learned pieces that they learned regularly in school. Generally, there were little verses that had a good moral teaching to them, you know, and often they learned pieces like Little Boy Blue and so on from their literature books. And then they would, whoever got to know them the best and could speak them the best, they would have them - they would get to elocute them for their parents, for the - and join their parents at the program, the school program.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ever put on melodramers?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh yes, yes. Sometimes the young people of the community, if they were ambitious and felt the need of getting together, why they would have plays. And there was a publishing company called the Sam French Company, and they turned out regular line of plays to be put on by rural schools. It didn't take much scenery, it didn't take much costuming, and generally they were funny ones, you know, that would appeal to the audience and send you home laughing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the feed? They have big, big feed when you have a play party night?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, and they often had that. Yes, sometimes - sometimes they generally sandwiches and cookies and maybe cake and coffee, generally always coffee. And that would be sometimes the communities would get together maybe for New Year's Day. Christmas Day they didn't very often get together, but Thanksgiving and New Year's, many of the communities made a regular habit of putting on a big Thanksgiving dinner at the schoolhouse and everybody come. And New Year's was another favorite time for them all to get together and eat a big New Year's dinner and then dance in the evening and in the afternoon and evening and visit. And those were good things to do with roads drifted and snow blowing, and people were very limited in how far they could go for a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was it that decided if there was going to be a night party? Would it be the teacher?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I think generally it was the teacher that promoted it. That was part of their all -around bit of public relations, would be to say that the young folks had a chance to get together and visit. And she was done the official chaperone. She and the school board would give official chaperones for these parties and things. And that was kind of the way it worked out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they called the evening? An entertainment or a party?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, school entertainment. Sometimes they called it the school program. And sometimes it was just we're going down to the school for a little party. And sometimes there were women's clubs. Later on, there got to be women's clubs in the communities. And then the women's clubs were great at sponsoring some kind of a doings and entertaining their husbands and their friends and the young people. But in the very oldest times, in the very earliest beginnings, I think it was largely sponsored by the teachers. Another thing they did almost before, before I was a teacher in the community, was having taffy poles. And that was another chance for young people to get together and socialize. And it didn't matter if they were pulling candy or what they were doing. It was an opportunity to meet and get acquainted. So they served a real purpose, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people of all ages dance in the play party games?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes, they were. Well, it was mostly the young people. But it just depended on the community. And some of the communities, the old people came too. And they'd get out. Especially for the dance square dances. Like Virginia Reel and all the old swing them on the corner like you're swinging them on the gate. All the old dances, they were callers to be a caller. And then they generally had a violin and an organ, the school organ. And they'd have somebody who could play the fiddle. And they'd dance. And they did that from the beginning, I think, of Homestead Days. Because someone wrote a very lovely poem about the boy playing the fiddle. So she could dance on the punchy on floor. And a punch on floor, of course, is a sod floor. Where the sod's just been turned up. So that would be kind of hard dancing. But I guess they did it.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did they continue to have the school entertainments?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, until the schools were, yes, until the schools were consolidated. They did it up to the very last. And that's the one thing the rural people missed more than anything else. Was it took away the center of their social life. The life of the community had centered around the schools. The dancing. The square dancing. The play party games. The entertainments marking the seasons. The interest in what their children were doing. So all those things entered into it. Now here comes my... Now turn it off a minute. Here comes my sister and Tom. Well, sure. You can't do that for a couple of days. Oh, it doesn't matter. It doesn't make any sense. Record, sure. That's better put down that way because then you get it the way you say it. Uh -huh. Well, you would form a circle. Everybody holding hands. And then you would start and sing. Happy is the Miller boy who lives by the meal. The wheel goes round of its own free will. One hand in the hopper and the other in the sack. The lady stepped forward and the gents fall back. And that way you would change partners. And when you go around the circle again, say the same thing. Happy is the Miller boy who lives by the meal. The wheel goes round of its own free will. One hand in the hopper and the other in the sack. The lady stepped forward and the gents fall back. Then you change partners. But in the meantime, there'd be somebody who was it who would be in the middle. And he would jump in and try to grab a partner. Sold it. The one who lost his partner would have to come into the center. Then sometimes the call or the singers would change it like a lady stepped forward. You see, or sometimes a gent would step forward. Sometimes a lady would step forward. And that would give you a different partner. That was happy as a Miller boy. Let's see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else did we have?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, skip to the blue. You have that here already. I think you had partners for that. And you stood in a circle, but each had a partner. And you would and then somebody would be it that's in the middle. And that would be skip, skip, skip to the blue. Skip, skip, skip to the blue. Skip, skip, skip to the blue. Skip to the blue, my darling. And one girl in the skipper, she'd get out and skip around. And she stopped in front of the boyfriend. Oh, and she chose him. And then they swing. And then everybody's swaying. Uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so then gone again, what will I do? Gone again, what will I do? Gone again, what will I do?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Skip to the blue, my darling. That would be if they lost their partner, you see. And they had many, many verses. They'd make up verses too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now they lose your partner?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, somebody would come out from the center. You'd have this it person in the center who had no partner. And they would come out and get your partner from you. And then you would be at the end. And you have to get on and dance with somebody else. A similar one to that would be a pig in the parlor. And everybody formed in a circle, excepting one who has chosen to be the pig. And the pig had to stand in the middle of this circle. And then he sang, uh, uh, my father and mother were Irish. And I was Irish too. We bought a wee piggy for six pence and it was Irish too. We kept the pig in the parlor. We kept the pig in the parlor. We kept the pig in the parlor. And it was Irish too. Now at a certain spot there, you all changed partners.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, or did you do a grand right and left?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yes. That's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's a grand right and left?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Get up here, dad. And we'll show them what a grand right and left is. Same thing as it is in square dances. Uh -huh. You stand the circle.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Get up.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And we'll roll. We'll get the girls.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Your right hand to... Well, your right hand to your right. Your right hand to your right. Your left hand to your neighbor. Yeah. Oh, your right hand to your right. Roll it right. Yeah. Your left hand to your neighbor.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And the right hand to the next to me. To the all from the neighbor. Oh, we were... I see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: We have to take care of the bottom. We did it all over all because the girls could always... We were standing wrong. Oh, yeah. Yeah. The girls would go this way. I did it. You would go that way. Oh, yeah. With your hand next. Oh, yes. Uh -huh. With your right hand. Dear partner, you take your right hand. And you go inside of her. I would go behind you and take her. You call that a grand right and left. She's my neighbor. The one you write. And for anybody who gets mixed up, then you sing it. And you just sing to them your right hand to your partner. Your left hand to your neighbor. The third one is your partner. And we'll all promise. That's when there's four in the... Aha. Oh, yeah. Yeah. So you choose a new partner. This would be singing. No instruments. No. Oh, it could be both. But usually just singing. Yes. Now, this man who led it in our community, he just sang with a good loud voice. And we all followed after everybody tried to sing. Of course, a lot of square dances were sung, too. Yes. A lot of square dances were sung. Well, they figured that this was like this kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These ones that we're just talking about, would these be dances or would these be games?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: We call them games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And if not, you'll find, was this me?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: There's a little difference. In the country communities, some objected to dancing. But if you called them games, you got by with them, you see. But others didn't care. And then you had the square dances. Well... There's really very little difference.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: A game usually had an it. And the dances, usually just changing of partners, was granted for partners. Do you remember...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One that I wrote down looked interesting to me, was water, water, wine, flour? Do you ever hear that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: No. No, that's a strange one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, are you researching games? Is that what you're interested in?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, we're talking about the old times in the county. In general. For the museum in general, yes. But this is something that I know Lola knows about. The one that she's been talking about would be the hills. Yeah, not around. That wildflower, wildflower growing up so high, something sorry, surely she must die.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: But they sent someone to see her and she revived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well, what will they do for her?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Wildflower, wildflower growing up so high, who's so sorry, so sorry, a little so and so must die.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What shall we do for her?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: We will send Sam Shrager to see her, see her, see her. We'll send Sam Shrager to see her and she'll be all right now. That would be more that the kids would play because I never saw that played in the games at the schoolhouse where the adults were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the kids and adults both play would just be the adults who played the party games?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Both would get out and play at the party games. Largely the young adults. But anybody would come and play. And lots of times they'd take a young adult, he would see some oldish lady and he'd run and get her and bring her out and she'd have fun. Lots of times the little kids rock. Well, they did pretty well mixed up. The doors came as well as the. Uh -huh. There was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could they be played outdoors?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: There was a drop by after chief of course. Oh yeah, this one that Lola names is, the little children would often pay that, play that one outdoors about wildflower, wildflower. Now, that's the first time I ever heard that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's dropped the hand?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Oh boy, that's an outside game. Well, you can play it inside too. We did. Yes, you could. Especially if you had to get a piece in. You all get together in a circle or a ring.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And there is an it. Yeah, this. And it comes around the outside with a hand for chief. Drops it behind somebody and somebody is supposed to chase them and catch them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happens?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, it goes like this.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: If they get tear around the circle before she gets caught, then the guy that has the hand, which before that was dropped behind, is in. Right. But if he catches her before, then he goes back in his own space and she has to be in. She has to be in. Uh -huh.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: A simple game. But that's an outdoors game mostly for kids. It dropped the handkerchief for kids. I never saw adults joining that much. Very much of the time. We've part of games usually be at the schoolhouse. Yes, they would always be. Then there's one called post office. You do that, Tommy. You get a kick out of everything. I never played post office. I never did know. No, they didn't believe in kissing in my neighborhood. And that came in to post office. They questioned Abby about it. She couldn't give them love. Well, if you had to have sold you and made a letter, you went to go make a letter. And one stamp, it took one stamp. That was one kiss. And if it was something else, I took two stamps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many stamps is this one?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: This one, they gave me. But you got special deliveries. Yeah, special deliveries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you have to go in and see the postmaster?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Yeah, somebody was postmaster.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they in a darkened place?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: I don't know. I never end up. My schools wouldn't stand for that kind of stuff. No, they wouldn't mind either. No, my school wouldn't mind. They left out the best part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I have to tell you, the way I saw the kids play it, did you get a letter? Did you stamp it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: And they'd stand right on your foot. That's all there was to that part. Yeah, but what's... This I don't know about this other. Then there was, in all kinds of games, there was a thing of redeeming forfeits, you heard about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: In some game or other, if you lose, you have to put up some article, pocket knife, watch, pencil, whatever you had, for having lost, I guess.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde or Thomas Wahl or Elizabeth Wahl
words: Then when everybody had lost something, then you got to redeem your forfeit, and you got up there. Now then, I'll be... You can hold it over my head now. You tell me part of it. Heavy, heavy. Heavy, heavy, hang over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What shall we owner do to redeem it?
